title: BCL2 inhibits cytochrome c release (follicular lymphoma)
vignette: A 60-year-old man undergoes lymph node biopsy due to persistent cervical lymphadenopathy. Histologic examination reveals a population of small lymphoid cells arranged in a follicular pattern. The cells demonstrate overexpression of the BCL2 gene. The protein encoded by this gene normally inhibits which of the following processes?

clues.note: Painless, persistent lymphadenopathy in an older adult is a common presentation of lymphoma. Context that sets up the follicular histology.

clues.text: small lymphoid cells arranged in a follicular pattern
clues.note: Small B cells in a nodular/follicular architecture = follicular lymphoma, the classic t(14;18) lymphoma that overexpresses BCL2.

The t(14;18) translocation places BCL2 (chromosome 18) under the immunoglobulin heavy-chain promoter (chromosome 14), driving BCL2 overexpression. BCL2 is ANTIAPOPTOTIC - it blocks the intrinsic pathway, letting tumor cells evade death.

whatMatters: Follicular lymphoma -> t(14;18) -> BCL2 overexpression. BCL2 is an antiapoptotic protein that guards the mitochondrial (intrinsic) pathway by preventing the proapoptotic factor cytochrome c from leaking out of the mitochondria. So the process BCL2 normally INHIBITS is cytochrome c release. Answer A.
strategy: When a stem hands you a protein and asks what it 'normally inhibits,' recall its pathway role. BCL2 = antiapoptotic guardian of the mitochondrial outer membrane; it prevents cytochrome c release (which would trigger caspase activation). The wrong options each belong to a DIFFERENT gene class: mismatch repair (MSH2), Rb/transcription, RAS/NF1, telomerase.

eliminations.label: DNA mismatch accumulation - mismatch repair genes (MSH2/MLH1)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: mismatch-repair proteins correct base-pairing errors; their loss causes microsatellite instability and Lynch syndrome (HNPCC). ❌ WHY IT FAILS: that is a repair function unrelated to BCL2's antiapoptotic role. ✅ Correct if: familial colon/endometrial cancer with microsatellite instability.

eliminations.label: Nuclear transcription - Rb tumor suppressor
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: active Rb binds E2F to block transcription and the G1->S transition. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: cell-cycle/transcription control is Rb's job, not BCL2's. ✅ Correct if: retinoblastoma/osteosarcoma, E2F, the G1/S checkpoint.

eliminations.label: RAS-induced signal transmission - RAS/NF1 pathway
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: RAS relays growth signals through MAP kinase; neurofibromin (NF1) is the GAP that inhibits RAS. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: BCL2 doesn't regulate RAS signaling. ✅ Correct if: neurofibromatosis or a constitutively active RAS oncogene.

eliminations.label: Telomere shortening - telomerase
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: telomerase (a reverse transcriptase) extends telomeres; reactivation lets cancer cells avoid replicative senescence. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: telomere maintenance is telomerase's role, not BCL2's. ✅ Correct if: asked how tumors achieve replicative immortality.

reasoning: Older adult, painless cervical nodes, small B cells in a follicular pattern = follicular lymphoma
Follicular lymphoma carries t(14;18) -> BCL2 overexpression
BCL2 is antiapoptotic: it guards the mitochondrial outer membrane
It normally prevents cytochrome c from leaking out (which would start caspase-driven apoptosis) -> answer A
mechanism: BCL2 is an antiapoptotic regulator of the intrinsic (mitochondrial) apoptosis pathway. The BCL2 family controls permeability of the mitochondrial outer membrane: proapoptotic members (BAX, BAK) form pores that let cytochrome c escape into the cytosol, where it nucleates the apoptosome and activates caspase-9, whereas antiapoptotic members (BCL2, BCL-XL) preserve membrane integrity and prevent cytochrome c release. In follicular lymphoma, the t(14;18) translocation fuses BCL2 on chromosome 18 to the immunoglobulin heavy-chain locus on chromosome 14, causing BCL2 overexpression; the neoplastic B cells fail to undergo apoptosis and accumulate. Thus the process BCL2 normally inhibits is the release of cytochrome c from mitochondria.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Path > apoptosis: intrinsic (mitochondrial) pathway; BCL2/BCL-XL antiapoptotic (prevent cytochrome c release) vs BAX/BAK proapoptotic; follicular lymphoma t(14;18) overexpresses BCL2.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: 'Normally inhibits which process' = recall BCL2's antiapoptotic action: blocking cytochrome c release. Don't overthink the lymphoma diagnosis (already implied).

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on apoptosis (intrinsic vs extrinsic), cytochrome c, caspases, BAX/BAK vs BCL2, t(14;18), or follicular lymphoma? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: The other options are real cancer mechanisms but belong to other genes (MSH2 mismatch repair, Rb transcription, RAS/NF1, telomerase). Matching 'cancer gene' to the wrong function is the trap.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: BCL2 = antiapoptotic, blocks cytochrome c release; follicular lymphoma = t(14;18).

keywords.back: Cytochrome c release from mitochondria (intrinsic apoptosis)
BAX/BAK (pore, release cytochrome c) vs BCL2/BCL-XL (preserve membrane)

firstAid.detail: Path > intrinsic pathway; antiapoptotic BCL2/BCL-XL prevent cytochrome c release; follicular lymphoma t(14;18).
firstAid.location: Path > intrinsic pathway; antiapoptotic BCL2/BCL-XL prevent cytochrome c release; follicular lymphoma t(14;18).

firstAid.detail: Path > mismatch repair (MSH2/MLH1, Lynch), Rb (transcription/G1-S), RAS/NF1, telomerase.
firstAid.location: Path > mismatch repair (MSH2/MLH1, Lynch), Rb (transcription/G1-S), RAS/NF1, telomerase.

firstAid.detail: Heme/Onc > nodular small-cell B lymphoma, t(14;18), BCL2-positive, indolent.
firstAid.location: Heme/Onc > nodular small-cell B lymphoma, t(14;18), BCL2-positive, indolent.

memoryHook: BCL2 = the cork in the mitochondria. It keeps cytochrome c bottled up so the cell can't trigger apoptosis - over-corked in follicular lymphoma's t(14;18).
